feedback.text: In general, I support the Digital Fairness Act but I would like the DFA to also have protections enshrined for video games as well as other software that was previously paid for to be able to continuously function. There have been instances where purchased software which relied on a server would be abandoned by the developers and the server would be shut down, rendering the client software inoperable. I propose that a developer / software publisher be mandated to support software for a minimum number of years (I suggest at least 20 years) but once the server is no longer to be supported, then I propose the server software and it's source code be released to the public so that others (or even possibly myself) can continue to maintain and run the server side software to insure that the client software I paid for will continue to function. The client software would also need to have a way to change the pointer to the replacement address of the server since the original domain name would likely be abandoned and so we would need a way for the client to communicate with the replacement network address of the server side software. Alternatively, just prior to the end of life of the client and server software, a software update should be published which would make these changes or roll the server side software into the client so that a server would no longer be needed and everything would just run locally on the client computer system. These are just some suggestions I have to make sure paid software can be reasonably made to continue to function. But the spirit of the idea would be for software publishers and developers to make reasonable efforts to allow people to continue to run their purchased software long after the developers decide to no longer support the code. I would like the DFA to address this somehow. I want to be able to keep using my purchased software as long as possible despite the creators of the software no longer supporting the infrastructure.
